question:
Is there a finite abelian group $G$ such that the product of the
orders of all its elements is $2^{2009}$?
solution:
No, there is no such group.
By the structure theorem for finitely generated abelian groups,
$G$ can be written as a product of cyclic groups.
If any of these factors has odd order, then $G$ has an element of odd order,
so the product of the orders of all of its elements cannot be a power of 2.

We may thus consider only abelian $2$-groups hereafter.
For such a group $G$, the product of the orders of all of its elements
has the form $2^{k(G)}$ for some nonnegative integer $G$, and we must show
that it is impossible to achieve $k(G) = 2009$.
Again by the structure theorem, we may write
\[
G \cong \prod_{i=1}^\infty (\ZZ/2^i \ZZ)^{e_i}
\]
for some nonnegative integers $e_1,e_2,\dots$, all but finitely many of
which are $0$.

For any nonnegative integer $m$, the elements of $G$ of order at most $2^m$
form a subgroup isomorphic to
\[
\prod_{i=1}^\infty (\ZZ/2^{\min\{i,m\}} \ZZ)^{e_i},
\]
which has $2^{s_m}$ elements for $s_m = \sum_{i=1}^\infty \min\{i,m\} e_i$.
Hence
\[
k(G) = \sum_{i=1}^\infty i(2^{s_i} - 2^{s_{i-1}}).
\]
Since $s_1 \leq s_2 \leq \cdots$, $k(G)+1$ is always divisible by $2^{s_1}$.
In particular, $k(G) = 2009$ forces $s_1 \leq 1$.

However, the only cases where $s_1 \leq 1$ are where all of the $e_i$ are $0$,
in which case $k(G) = 0$, or where $e_i = 1$ for some $i$ and $e_j = 0$
for $j \neq i$, in which case $k(G) = (i-1)2^i + 1$.
The right side is a strictly increasing function
of $i$ which equals $1793$ for $i=8$ and $4097$ for $i=9$, so it can never equal
2009. This proves the claim.

\textbf{Remark.} One can also arrive at the key congruence by dividing $G$
into equivalence classes, by declaring two elements to be equivalent if they generate the
same cyclic subgroup of $G$.
For $h>0$, an element of order $2^h$ belongs to an equivalence class of size $2^{h-1}$,
so the products of the orders of the elements of this equivalence class is $2^j$
for $j = h 2^{h-1}$. This quantity is divisible by 4 as long as $h > 1$;
thus to have $k(G) \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, the number of elements of $G$ of order 2 must be
congruent to 1 modulo 4. However, there are exactly $2^e-1$ such elements,
for $e$ the number of cyclic factors of $G$. Hence $e = 1$, and one concludes as in the
given solution.

descriptive_long.question:
Is there a finite abelian group $totalgrp$ such that the product of the
orders of all its elements is $2^{2009}$?
descriptive_long.solution:
No, there is no such group.
By the structure theorem for finitely generated abelian groups,
$totalgrp$ can be written as a product of cyclic groups.
If any of these factors has odd order, then $totalgrp$ has an element of odd order,
so the product of the orders of all of its elements cannot be a power of 2.

We may thus consider only abelian $2$-groups hereafter.
For such a group $totalgrp$, the product of the orders of all of its elements
has the form $2^{orderpower(totalgrp)}$ for some nonnegative integer $totalgrp$, and we must show
that it is impossible to achieve $orderpower(totalgrp) = 2009$.
Again by the structure theorem, we may write
\[
totalgrp \cong \prod_{indexprim=1}^\infty (\mathbb{Z}/2^{indexprim} \mathbb{Z})^{expindex}
\]
for some nonnegative integers $expbase_1,expbase_2,\dots$, all but finitely many of
which are $0$.

For any nonnegative integer $boundind$, the elements of $totalgrp$ of order at most $2^{boundind}$
form a subgroup isomorphic to
\[
\prod_{indexprim=1}^\infty (\mathbb{Z}/2^{\min\{indexprim,boundind\}} \mathbb{Z})^{expindex},
\]
which has $2^{sumbound}$ elements for $sumbound = \sum_{indexprim=1}^\infty \min\{indexprim,boundind\} expindex$.
Hence
\[
orderpower(totalgrp) = \sum_{indexprim=1}^\infty indexprim(2^{sumindex} - 2^{sumprev}).
\]
Since $sumfirst \leq sumindex \leq \cdots$, $orderpower(totalgrp)+1$ is always divisible by $2^{sumfirst}$.
In particular, $orderpower(totalgrp) = 2009$ forces $sumfirst \leq 1$.

However, the only cases where $sumfirst \leq 1$ are where all of the $expindex$ are $0$,
in which case $orderpower(totalgrp) = 0$, or where $expindex = 1$ for some $indexprim$ and $expalt = 0$
for $indexsec \neq indexprim$, in which case $orderpower(totalgrp) = (indexprim-1)2^{indexprim} + 1$.
The right side is a strictly increasing function
of $indexprim$ which equals $1793$ for $indexprim=8$ and $4097$ for $indexprim=9$, so it can never equal
2009. This proves the claim.

\textbf{Remark.} One can also arrive at the key congruence by dividing $totalgrp$
into equivalence classes, by declaring two elements to be equivalent if they generate the
same cyclic subgroup of $totalgrp$.
For $heightvar>0$, an element of order $2^{heightvar}$ belongs to an equivalence class of size $2^{heightvar-1}$,
so the products of the orders of the elements of this equivalence class is $2^{indexsec}$
for $indexsec = heightvar 2^{heightvar-1}$. This quantity is divisible by 4 as long as $heightvar > 1$;
thus to have $orderpower(totalgrp) \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, the number of elements of $totalgrp$ of order 2 must be
congruent to 1 modulo 4. However, there are exactly $2^{expbase}-1$ such elements,
for $expbase$ the number of cyclic factors of $totalgrp$. Hence $expbase = 1$, and one concludes as in the
given solution.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Is there a finite abelian group $dawncircle$ such that the product of the
orders of all its elements is $2^{2009}$?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
No, there is no such group.
By the structure theorem for finitely generated abelian groups,
$dawncircle$ can be written as a product of cyclic groups.
If any of these factors has odd order, then $dawncircle$ has an element of odd order,
so the product of the orders of all of its elements cannot be a power of 2.

We may thus consider only abelian $2$-groups hereafter.
For such a group $dawncircle$, the product of the orders of all of its elements
has the form $2^{mistheight(dawncircle)}$ for some nonnegative integer $dawncircle$, and we must show
that it is impossible to achieve $mistheight(dawncircle) = 2009$.
Again by the structure theorem, we may write
\[
dawncircle \cong \prod_{riverstone=1}^\infty (\ZZ/2^{riverstone} \ZZ)^{echochamber}
\]
for some nonnegative integers $galefactor_1,galefactor_2,\dots$, all but finitely many of
which are $0$.

For any nonnegative integer $windyroad$, the elements of $dawncircle$ of order at most $2^{windyroad}$
form a subgroup isomorphic to
\[
\prod_{riverstone=1}^\infty (\ZZ/2^{\min\{riverstone,windyroad\}} \ZZ)^{echochamber},
\]
which has $2^{embertrail}$ elements for $embertrail = \sum_{riverstone=1}^\infty \min\{riverstone,windyroad\} echochamber$.
Hence
\[
mistheight(dawncircle) = \sum_{riverstone=1}^\infty riverstone(2^{shadowpath} - 2^{quietvalley}).
\]
Since $silentbrook \leq s_2 \leq \cdots$, $mistheight(dawncircle)+1$ is always divisible by $2^{silentbrook}$.
In particular, $mistheight(dawncircle) = 2009$ forces $silentbrook \leq 1$.

However, the only cases where $silentbrook \leq 1$ are where all of the $echochamber$ are $0$,
in which case $mistheight(dawncircle) = 0$, or where $echochamber = 1$ for some $riverstone$ and $hollowcrest = 0$
for $willowseed \neq riverstone$, in which case $mistheight(dawncircle) = (riverstone-1)2^{riverstone} + 1$.
The right side is a strictly increasing function
of $riverstone$ which equals $1793$ for $riverstone=8$ and $4097$ for $riverstone=9$, so it can never equal
2009. This proves the claim.

\textbf{Remark.} One can also arrive at the key congruence by dividing $dawncircle$
into equivalence classes, by declaring two elements to be equivalent if they generate the
same cyclic subgroup of $dawncircle$.
For $cloudgate>0$, an element of order $2^{cloudgate}$ belongs to an equivalence class of size $2^{cloudgate-1}$,
so the products of the orders of the elements of this equivalence class is $2^{willowseed}$
for $willowseed = cloudgate 2^{cloudgate-1}$. This quantity is divisible by 4 as long as $cloudgate > 1$;
thus to have $mistheight(dawncircle) \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, the number of elements of $dawncircle$ of order 2 must be
congruent to 1 modulo 4. However, there are exactly $2^{galefactor}-1$ such elements,
for $galefactor$ the number of cyclic factors of $dawncircle$. Hence $galefactor = 1$, and one concludes as in the
given solution.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Is there a finite abelian group $disordering$ such that the product of the
orders of all its elements is $2^{2009}$?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
No, there is no such group.
By the structure theorem for finitely generated abelian groups,
$disordering$ can be written as a product of cyclic groups.
If any of these factors has odd order, then $disordering$ has an element of odd order,
so the product of the orders of all of its elements cannot be a power of 2.

We may thus consider only abelian $2$-groups hereafter.
For such a group $disordering$, the product of the orders of all of its elements
has the form $2^{voidvalue(disordering)}$ for some nonnegative integer $disordering$, and we must show
that it is impossible to achieve $voidvalue(disordering) = 2009$.
Again by the structure theorem, we may write
\[
disordering \cong \prod_{wholeindex=1}^\infty (\ZZ/2^{wholeindex} \ZZ)^{shrinkidx}
\]
for some nonnegative integers $shrinkidx_{1},shrinkidx_{2},\dots$, all but finitely many of
which are $0$.

For any nonnegative integer $negativezone$, the elements of $disordering$ of order at most $2^{negativezone}$
form a subgroup isomorphic to
\[
\prod_{wholeindex=1}^\infty (\ZZ/2^{\min\{wholeindex,negativezone\}} \ZZ)^{shrinkidx},
\]
which has $2^{gapmeasure}$ elements for $gapmeasure = \sum_{wholeindex=1}^\infty \min\{wholeindex,negativezone\} shrinkidx$.
Hence
\[
voidvalue(disordering) = \sum_{wholeindex=1}^\infty wholeindex(2^{splitvalue} - 2^{splitminus}).
\]
Since $splitone \leq splitvalue_{2} \leq \cdots$, $voidvalue(disordering)+1$ is always divisible by $2^{splitone}$.
In particular, $voidvalue(disordering) = 2009$ forces $splitone \leq 1$.

However, the only cases where $splitone \leq 1$ are where all of the $shrinkidx$ are $0$,
in which case $voidvalue(disordering) = 0$, or where $shrinkidx = 1$ for some $wholeindex$ and $shrinkjidx = 0$
for $singularone \neq wholeindex$, in which case $voidvalue(disordering) = (wholeindex-1)2^{wholeindex} + 1$.
The right side is a strictly increasing function
of $wholeindex$ which equals $1793$ for $wholeindex=8$ and $4097$ for $wholeindex=9$, so it can never equal
2009. This proves the claim.

\textbf{Remark.} One can also arrive at the key congruence by dividing $disordering$
into equivalence classes, by declaring two elements to be equivalent if they generate the
same cyclic subgroup of $disordering$.
For $shallowness>0$, an element of order $2^{shallowness}$ belongs to an equivalence class of size $2^{shallowness-1}$,
so the products of the orders of the elements of this equivalence class is $2^{singularone}$
for $singularone = shallowness 2^{shallowness-1}$. This quantity is divisible by 4 as long as $shallowness > 1$;
thus to have $voidvalue(disordering) \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, the number of elements of $disordering$ of order 2 must be
congruent to 1 modulo 4. However, there are exactly $2^{shrinker}-1$ such elements,
for $shrinker$ the number of cyclic factors of $disordering$. Hence $shrinker = 1$, and one concludes as in the
given solution.

\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
P(G)=\prod_{g\in G}\operatorname{ord}(g)
\]
be the product of the orders of all elements of a finite abelian group $G$, and put  
\[
\mathcal{K}_{2}=\Bigl\{k\ge 0:\,\exists \text{ finite abelian }G\text{ with }P(G)=2^{\,k}\Bigr\}.
\]

Throughout, finite abelian $2$-groups are written additively.  
If  
\[
G\cong\bigoplus_{j=1}^{t}\mathbf{Z}/2^{\lambda_{j}}\mathbf{Z}
\quad(\lambda_{1}\ge\lambda_{2}\ge\dots\ge\lambda_{t}\ge 1),
\]
define multiplicities  
\[
e_{m}(G)=\bigl|\{\,j\mid\lambda_{j}\ge m\,\}\bigr|\qquad(m\ge 1),
\]
and partial sums  
\[
S_{m}(G)=\sum_{q=1}^{m}e_{q}(G)\qquad(1\le m\le\lambda_{1}).\tag{$\dagger$}
\]
Thus $0<S_{1}(G)<\dots<S_{\lambda_{1}}(G)$.

A strictly increasing sequence  
\[
0<a_{1}<a_{2}<\dots<a_{r}\tag{$\ast$}
\]
is called $2$-admissible if its consecutive gaps  
\[
d_{i}=a_{i}-a_{i-1}\qquad(a_{0}:=0)\tag{$\ast\ast$}
\]
satisfy $d_{1}\ge d_{2}\ge\dots\ge d_{r}>0$.

1.  Show that for every $k>0$
\[
k\in\mathcal{K}_{2}\Longleftrightarrow
\exists \text{ $2$-admissible }(a_{1},\dots ,a_{r})\text{ with }
k=r\cdot 2^{a_{r}}-\bigl(1+2^{a_{1}}+\dots+2^{a_{r-1}}\bigr).\tag{$\star$}
\]
(The exponent $k=0$ is realised by the trivial group.)  
Moreover prove that the assignments  
\[
G\longmapsto\bigl(S_{1}(G),\dots,S_{\lambda_{1}}(G)\bigr),\qquad
(a_{1},\dots ,a_{r})\stackrel{(\star)}{\longmapsto}k
\]
give bijections
\[
\bigl\{\text{isomorphism classes of non-trivial }2\text{-groups}\bigr\}
\longleftrightarrow
\bigl\{\text{$2$-admissible sequences}\bigr\}
\longleftrightarrow
\mathcal{K}_{2}\setminus\{0\}.
\]

2.  For every admissible $k$ determine the number of pairwise non-isomorphic finite abelian groups $G$ with $P(G)=2^{\,k}$, and decide whether this number can ever exceed $1$.

3.  Decide whether $7001\in\mathcal{K}_{2}$; if the answer is affirmative, state how many non-isomorphic groups realise this value and describe them up to isomorphism.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $v_{2}(\,\cdot\,)$ denotes the $2$-adic valuation; all groups are written additively.

\textbf{A.  Invariant factors, multiplicities and a fundamental formula.}  
By the structure theorem every finite abelian $2$-group has a decomposition  
\[
G\cong\bigoplus_{j=1}^{t}\mathbf{Z}/2^{\lambda_{j}}\mathbf{Z}
\quad(\lambda_{1}\ge\dots\ge\lambda_{t}\ge 1).\tag{1}
\]
Put $h=\lambda_{1}$ and define  
\[
e_{m}=\#\{\,j\mid\lambda_{j}\ge m\,\}\quad(1\le m\le h),\qquad
S_{m}=\sum_{q=1}^{m}e_{q},\qquad S_{0}=0.\tag{2}
\]
Then $0<S_{1}<\dots<S_{h}$ and $S_{h}=t$.  
The subgroup $G[2^{m}]$ of elements annihilated by $2^{m}$ has order $2^{S_{m}}$; hence there are $2^{S_{m}}-2^{S_{m-1}}$ elements of exact order $2^{m}$.  
Taking the product of element orders gives
\[
k(G)=\sum_{m=1}^{h}m\bigl(2^{S_{m}}-2^{S_{m-1}}\bigr)
      =h\cdot 2^{S_{h}}-\sum_{m=0}^{h-1}2^{S_{m}}.\tag{3}
\]

\textbf{B.  From a $2$-group to a $2$-admissible sequence.}  
Set $a_{i}=S_{i}\;(1\le i\le h)$.  
Because $e_{i}=S_{i}-S_{i-1}>0$ and $e_{i+1}\le e_{i}$, the gaps $d_{i}=a_{i}-a_{i-1}=e_{i}$ are weakly decreasing and positive, so  
\[
(a_{1},\dots ,a_{h}) \text{ is $2$-admissible.}
\]
Equation (3) is precisely formula $(\star)$ with $r=h$.  Thus every non-trivial $2$-group yields a $2$-admissible sequence and an exponent $k$ with $2^{k}=P(G)$.

\textbf{C.  From a $2$-admissible sequence to a $2$-group.}  
Conversely, let $(a_{1},\dots ,a_{r})$ be $2$-admissible and put
\[
e_{m}=a_{m}-a_{m-1}\quad(1\le m\le r).
\]
The sequence $(e_{1},\dots ,e_{r})$ is strictly positive and weakly decreasing, hence there exists a unique list  
\[
\lambda_{1}\ge\dots\ge\lambda_{t}\ge 1
\quad(t=e_{1})
\]
given by
\[
\lambda_{j}=\max\{\,m\mid e_{m}\ge j\,\}\qquad(1\le j\le t).\tag{4}
\]
With these $\lambda_{j}$ formula (1) defines a $2$-group $G$ for which $S_{m}=a_{m}$ and, by (3), $k(G)=k$ from $(\star)$.  
Hence
\[
\{\text{non-trivial }2\text{-groups}\}\twoheadrightarrow
\{\text{$2$-admissible sequences}\}\twoheadrightarrow
\mathcal{K}_{2}\setminus\{0\}\tag{5}
\]
are both surjections.

\textbf{D.  Injectivity of the map $(\ast)\mapsto k$.}  
Let  
\[
A=(a_{1},\dots ,a_{r}),\qquad
B=(b_{1},\dots ,b_{s})
\]
be $2$-admissible and assume $k(A)=k(B)=k$.  We prove $A=B$.

\emph{Step 1: the first entry is determined by $k$.}  
Because
\[
k+1=r\cdot 2^{a_{r}}-\sum_{i=1}^{r-1}2^{a_{i}}
          =s\cdot 2^{b_{s}}-\sum_{j=1}^{s-1}2^{b_{j}},
\]
every term on the right is divisible by $2^{a_{1}}$ (respectively $2^{b_{1}}$).  
Hence
\[
v_{2}(k+1)=a_{1}=b_{1}.\tag{6}
\]

\emph{Step 2: reduction to shorter sequences.}  
Put
\[
k^{\prime}:=\frac{k+1-2^{a_{1}}}{2^{a_{1}}}
           =r\cdot 2^{a_{r}-a_{1}}-\sum_{i=2}^{r-1}2^{a_{i}-a_{1}}
           =s\cdot 2^{b_{s}-a_{1}}-\sum_{j=2}^{s-1}2^{b_{j}-a_{1}}.\tag{7}
\]
Set
\[
A^{\prime}=(a_{2}-a_{1},\dots ,a_{r}-a_{1}),\qquad
B^{\prime}=(b_{2}-a_{1},\dots ,b_{s}-a_{1}).
\]
Because the original gaps $d_{i}$ are weakly decreasing, the new sequences $A^{\prime}$ and $B^{\prime}$ are again $2$-admissible (possibly empty).  
Moreover $k^{\prime}+1$ is obtained from $A^{\prime}$ and $B^{\prime}$ via $(\star)$ exactly as $k+1$ was obtained from $A$ and $B$.

\emph{Step 3: induction.}  
Iterating the construction of Step 2 we build successive quotients
\[
k_{0}+1=k+1,\quad
k_{1}+1=k^{\prime}+1,\quad
k_{2}+1,\dots
\]
and successively shorter $2$-admissible sequences; on each level the first entry is determined by the $2$-adic valuation, by (6).  
The process terminates when the sequence becomes empty, showing that \emph{each entry of $A$ and of $B$ is uniquely determined by $k$}.  Hence $A=B$.

\emph{Alternative direct check for the last entry.}  
If one prefers to stay on one level, it suffices to distinguish three cases.

\smallskip
\noindent
(i) $r\neq s$.  
Assume $r<s$ and $a_{i}=b_{i}$ for $i\le r$.  Then
\[
k+1=r\cdot 2^{a_{r}}-\!\!\sum_{i=1}^{r-1}\!\!2^{a_{i}}
     <s\cdot 2^{b_{s}}-\!\!\sum_{j=1}^{s-1}\!\!2^{b_{j}}=k+1,
\]
a contradiction.

\smallskip
\noindent
(ii) $r=s$ and $t<r$ is the first index with $a_{t}\neq b_{t}$.  
Exactly as in the original solution one reduces modulo $2^{a_{t}+1}$ to obtain a contradiction.

\smallskip
\noindent
(iii) $r=s$ and the sequences differ \emph{only} in the last entry.  
If $a_{r}<b_{r}$, then
\[
k+1=r\cdot 2^{a_{r}}-\sum_{i=1}^{r-1}2^{a_{i}}
     <r\cdot 2^{b_{r}}-\sum_{i=1}^{r-1}2^{a_{i}}=k+1,
\]
again impossible.  Thus $a_{r}=b_{r}$.  

\smallskip
Either way the map $(\ast)\mapsto k$ is injective.  Combined with (5) we have the bijections
\[
\bigl\{\text{non-trivial }2\text{-groups}\bigr\}
\longleftrightarrow
\bigl\{\text{$2$-admissible sequences}\bigr\}
\longleftrightarrow
\mathcal{K}_{2}\setminus\{0\}.\tag{8}
\]

\textbf{E.  A convenient $2$-adic test.}  
Putting $m=0$ in (3) yields
\[
k+1=h\cdot 2^{S_{h}}-\sum_{m=1}^{h-1}2^{S_{m}}.\tag{9}
\]
All summands are divisible by $2^{S_{1}}$, while the quotient is odd, so
\[
v_{2}(k+1)=S_{1}=e_{1}=t,\tag{10}
\]
the number of invariant factors of every group realising $k$.

\textbf{F.  Counting isomorphism classes for a fixed $k$.}  
Because of (8) each $k\in\mathcal{K}_{2}\setminus\{0\}$ originates from a \emph{unique} $2$-admissible sequence and therefore from a \emph{unique} structure sequence $(\lambda_{1},\dots ,\lambda_{t})$.  
Consequently
\[
\forall\,k\in\mathcal{K}_{2}\qquad
\bigl|\{\,G\mid P(G)=2^{\,k}\}/\!\!\cong\bigr|=
\begin{cases}
1,&k>0,\\
1,&k=0 \text{ (the trivial group).}
\end{cases}\tag{11}
\]
The required number never exceeds $1$.

\textbf{G.  The cyclic case.}  
For $t=1$ we have $\lambda=(h)$ with $h\ge 1$ and $S_{m}=\min\{m,h\}$.  Substituting into (3) gives
\[
k=(h-1)\cdot 2^{h}+1\qquad(h\ge 1).\tag{12}
\]

\textbf{H.  The test value $k=7001$.}  
Compute $k+1=7002=2\cdot 3501$, so $v_{2}(k+1)=1$.  
By (10) any realising group would have $t=1$ and hence be cyclic; thus (12) must satisfy
\[
(h-1)\cdot 2^{h}=7000.\tag{13}
\]
Since $v_{2}(7000)=3$, necessarily $h\le 3$.  Direct inspection
\[
h=1\Longrightarrow 0,\qquad
h=2\Longrightarrow 4,\qquad
h=3\Longrightarrow 16,
\]
shows that (13) has no solution.  Therefore
\[
7001\notin\mathcal{K}_{2}.\tag{14}
\]

\textbf{I.  Final answers.}  

(1)  For $k>0$, $k\in\mathcal{K}_{2}$ iff there is a $2$-admissible sequence satisfying $(\star)$; the correspondences in (8) are bijective.

(2)  For every $k\in\mathcal{K}_{2}$ exactly \emph{one} finite abelian group (up to isomorphism) satisfies $P(G)=2^{\,k}$; the number never exceeds $1$.

(3)  One has $7001\notin\mathcal{K}_{2}$; hence no finite abelian group realises the value $2^{7001}$.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
P(G)=\prod_{g\in G}\operatorname{ord}(g)
\]
be the product of the orders of all elements of a finite abelian group $G$, and put  
\[
\mathcal{K}_{2}\;=\;\Bigl\{k\ge 0\;:\;\exists\;\text{finite abelian }G\text{ with }P(G)=2^{\,k}\Bigr\}.
\]

Throughout, finite abelian $2$-groups are written additively.  
For such a group  
\[
G\cong\bigoplus_{j=1}^{t}\mathbf{Z}/2^{\lambda_{j}}\mathbf{Z}
\qquad(\lambda_{1}\ge\lambda_{2}\ge\dots\ge\lambda_{t}\ge 1),
\]
define the multiplicities  
\[
e_{m}(G):=\bigl|\{\,j\mid\lambda_{j}\ge m\,\}\bigr| \qquad(m\ge 1).
\]
The finite sequence $e_{1}(G),e_{2}(G),\dots ,e_{\lambda_{1}}(G)$ is strictly positive and weakly decreasing; conversely every such sequence occurs for a unique isomorphism class of $2$-groups.

Put  
\[
S_{m}(G):=\sum_{q=1}^{m}e_{q}(G)\qquad(1\le m\le\lambda_{1}).\tag{$\dagger$}
\]
Hence $0<S_{1}<\dots<S_{\lambda_{1}}$.

A strictly increasing finite sequence  
\[
0<a_{1}<a_{2}<\dots<a_{r}\tag{$\ast$}
\]
is called $2$-admissible if its consecutive gaps  
\[
d_{i}:=a_{i}-a_{i-1}\qquad(a_{0}:=0)\tag{$\ast\ast$}
\]
satisfy $d_{1}\ge d_{2}\ge\dots\ge d_{r}>0$.

1.\; Show that for every positive integer $k$ one has $k\in\mathcal{K}_{2}$ if and only if there exists  
   a $2$-admissible sequence $(\ast)$ such that  
   \[
   k=r\cdot 2^{a_{r}}-\bigl(1+2^{a_{1}}+2^{a_{2}}+\dots+2^{a_{r-1}}\bigr).\tag{$\star$}
   \]
   (The exponent $k=0$ is realised by the trivial group.)  
   Moreover prove that the assignments  
   \[
   G\longmapsto\bigl(S_{1}(G),\dots,S_{\lambda_{1}}(G)\bigr)\quad\text{and}\quad
   (a_{1},\dots,a_{r})\longmapsto k\text{ via }(\star)
   \]
   give bijections  
   \[
   \{\text{isomorphism classes of non-trivial }2\text{-groups}\}
   \;\longleftrightarrow\;
   \{\text{$2$-admissible sequences}\}
   \;\longleftrightarrow\;
   \mathcal{K}_{2}\setminus\{0\}.
   \]

2.\; For every admissible exponent $k$ determine the exact number of pairwise non-isomorphic finite abelian groups $G$ with $P(G)=2^{\,k}$, and decide whether this number can ever exceed $1$.

3.\; Decide whether $7001\in\mathcal{K}_{2}$; if the answer is affirmative, state how many non-isomorphic groups realise this value and describe them up to isomorphism.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $v_{2}(\,\cdot\,)$ denotes the $2$-adic valuation and all groups are written additively.

\textbf{A.  Invariant factors, multiplicities and the fundamental formula.}  
By the structure theorem every finite abelian $2$-group is  
\[
G\cong\bigoplus_{j=1}^{t}\mathbf{Z}/2^{\lambda_{j}}\mathbf{Z}
\qquad(\lambda_{1}\ge\dots\ge\lambda_{t}\ge 1).\tag{1}
\]
Put $h:=\lambda_{1}$ and define  
\[
e_{m}:=\#\{\,j\mid\lambda_{j}\ge m\,\}\quad(1\le m\le h),\qquad
S_{m}:=\sum_{q=1}^{m}e_{q},\qquad S_{0}:=0.\tag{2}
\]
Then $0<S_{1}<\dots<S_{h}$ and $S_{h}=t$.  
Let $G[2^{m}]$ be the subgroup of elements annihilated by $2^{m}$.  One has $\lvert G[2^{m}]\rvert=2^{S_{m}}$; hence the number of elements of exact order $2^{m}$ equals $2^{S_{m}}-2^{S_{m-1}}$.  
Multiplying the orders of all elements gives  
\[
k(G)=\sum_{m=1}^{h}m\bigl(2^{S_{m}}-2^{S_{m-1}}\bigr)
      =h\cdot 2^{S_{h}}-\sum_{m=0}^{h-1}2^{S_{m}}.\tag{3}
\]

\textbf{B.  From a $2$-group to a $2$-admissible sequence.}  
Put $a_{i}:=S_{i}\;(1\le i\le h)$.  
Because $e_{i}=S_{i}-S_{i-1}>0$ and $e_{i+1}\le e_{i}$, the gaps $d_{i}=a_{i}-a_{i-1}=e_{i}$ are weakly decreasing and positive.  Thus $\bigl(a_{1},\dots,a_{h}\bigr)$ is $2$-admissible.  
Equation (3) rewrites exactly as $(\star)$ with $r=h$.

\textbf{C.  From a $2$-admissible sequence to a $2$-group.}  
Conversely, let $\bigl(a_{1},\dots,a_{r}\bigr)$ be $2$-admissible and set $e_{m}:=a_{m}-a_{m-1}$ $(1\le m\le r)$.  
The weakly decreasing positive sequence $(e_{1},\dots,e_{r})$ satisfies (2) with $h:=r$.  
Define  
\[
\lambda_{j}:=\max\{\,m\mid e_{m}\ge j\,\}\quad(1\le j\le e_{1}).\tag{4}
\]
Then $(\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{t})$ as in (1) yields a $2$-group $G$ with $S_{m}=a_{m}$, and (3) gives $k(G)=k$ from $(\star)$.  
Hence we obtain a surjection  
\[
\{\text{isomorphism classes of non-trivial }2\text{-groups}\}\twoheadrightarrow
\mathcal{K}_{2}\setminus\{0\}.\tag{5}
\]

\textbf{D.  Uniqueness of the representation - injectivity of $(\star)$.}  
We prove that the map $(\ast)\mapsto k$ defined by $(\star)$ is injective.  

Assume two $2$-admissible sequences  
\[
A=\bigl(a_{1},\dots,a_{r}\bigr),\qquad
B=\bigl(b_{1},\dots,b_{s}\bigr)
\]
satisfy $(\star)$ with the same $k$.  
Let $t$ be the smallest index with $a_{t}\neq b_{t}$ and assume $a_{t}<b_{t}$; if no such $t$ exists the sequences coincide and we are done.

Write $k+1$ in the two $(\star)$-forms:
\[
k+1
=r\cdot 2^{a_{r}}-\sum_{i=1}^{r-1}2^{a_{i}}
=s\cdot 2^{b_{s}}-\sum_{j=1}^{s-1}2^{b_{j}}.\tag{6}
\]
Reduce (6) modulo $2^{a_{t}+1}$.

\emph{For the $A$-expression:}  
All terms $2^{a_{i}}$ with $i\ge t$ except $i=t$ are divisible by $2^{a_{t}+1}$, and $r\cdot 2^{a_{r}}$ is as well because $a_{r}>a_{t}$.  Hence
\[
k+1\equiv-2^{a_{t}}-\sum_{i=1}^{t-1}2^{a_{i}}\pmod{2^{a_{t}+1}}.\tag{7}
\]

\emph{For the $B$-expression:}  
Since $b_{t}\ge a_{t}+1$, every term on the right-hand side of the $B$-expression is divisible by $2^{a_{t}+1}$ except possibly those with $j<t$.  Consequently
\[
k+1\equiv-\sum_{i=1}^{t-1}2^{a_{i}}\pmod{2^{a_{t}+1}}.\tag{8}
\]

Subtracting (8) from (7) gives  
\[
0\equiv -2^{a_{t}}\pmod{2^{a_{t}+1}},
\]
a contradiction.  Therefore no two distinct $2$-admissible sequences can produce the same $k$, and the map $(\ast)\mapsto k$ is injective.  Together with the surjectivity proved in B-C we obtain the desired bijections:
\[
\{\text{isomorphism classes of non-trivial }2\text{-groups}\}
\;\longleftrightarrow\;
\{\text{$2$-admissible sequences}\}
\;\longleftrightarrow\;
\mathcal{K}_{2}\setminus\{0\}.\tag{9}
\]

\textbf{E.  A useful $2$-adic test.}  
Setting $m=0$ in (3) we have
\[
k+1=h\cdot 2^{S_{h}}-\sum_{m=1}^{h-1}2^{S_{m}}.\tag{10}
\]
All terms on the right-hand side are divisible by $2^{S_{1}}$, but the quotient is odd, so
\[
v_{2}(k+1)=S_{1}=e_{1}=t,\tag{11}
\]
the number of invariant factors of any realising group.

\textbf{F.  Counting isomorphism classes for a fixed $k$.}  
Because of (9) every positive $k\in\mathcal{K}_{2}$ corresponds to \emph{exactly one} $2$-admissible sequence and hence to \emph{exactly one} isomorphism class of finite abelian $2$-groups.  Adding the trivial group for $k=0$ yields
\[
\forall\,k\in\mathcal{K}_{2}\qquad
\bigl|\{\,G\mid P(G)=2^{\,k}\}/\!\!\cong\bigr|=1.\tag{12}
\]
Thus the required number never exceeds $1$.

\textbf{G.  The cyclic case.}  
For $t=1$ we have $\lambda=(h)$ $(h\ge 1)$ and $S_{m}=\min\{m,h\}$.  Substituting into (3) gives
\[
k=(h-1)\cdot 2^{h}+1\qquad(h\ge 1).\tag{13}
\]

\textbf{H.  The test value $k=7001$.}  
Compute $k+1=7002=2\cdot 3501$, so $v_{2}(k+1)=1$.  By (11) any realising group would have $t=1$ and therefore be cyclic; hence (13) must satisfy
\[
(h-1)\cdot 2^{h}=7000.\tag{14}
\]
Because $v_{2}(7000)=3$ one must have $h\le 3$.  Direct inspection
\[
h=1\;\Rightarrow\;0,\quad
h=2\;\Rightarrow\;4,\quad
h=3\;\Rightarrow\;16,
\]
shows (14) has no solution.  Therefore
\[
7001\notin\mathcal{K}_{2}.\tag{15}
\]

\textbf{I.  Final answers.}  
(1)  The set $\mathcal{K}_{2}\setminus\{0\}$ is in bijection with $2$-admissible sequences; every admissible $k$ possesses the unique representation $(\star)$.  

(2)  For every $k\in\mathcal{K}_{2}$ precisely one finite abelian group (up to isomorphism) satisfies $P(G)=2^{\,k}$; the number of such groups never exceeds $1$.  

(3)  One has $7001\notin\mathcal{K}_{2}$; consequently no finite abelian group realises the value $2^{7001}$.
